narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.70, LSAT score of 28, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 1 and 2 respectively, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 56.5% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward success because your 3.70 is notably higher than the typical 3.21 average, showing solid prior academic strength. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—the second-lowest group and well below the average of 5.53—had an even stronger counteracting effect, signaling to the model a significant dip in performance that raises concerns about sustaining momentum through law school and the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also much lower than the average of 5.71, added further weight toward the failure prediction, reinforcing worries about early academic struggles. While your LSAT score of 28 is below the typical 36.70 and your middle income aligns with many applicants, these played lesser roles compared to your decile rankings and GPA in tipping the scales. Full-time status, common for 93% of students, didn't sway things much either. In essence, the model saw your high undergrad GPA as a bright spot amid weaker school deciles, but the latter dominated, portraying higher bar failure risk than for typical admits whose deciles cluster around the middle of their classes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive environments. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds favorably, opening doors to programs that value your unique background and potential.
